name: Linear corridor layout
description: A single straight corridor runs the long axis of the floor plan, acting as the sole circulation spine from which all rooms branch off. Rooms may attach to one side (single-loaded corridor, typical of narrow buildings against an external wall) or to both sides (double-loaded corridor, maximising room count per unit footprint). The corridor is usually 2 blocks wide to allow two occupants to pass, and runs uninterrupted from one short end of the building to the other, often terminating in a window or door to admit natural light. This layout underlies the Roman atrium-flanked domus, monastic dormitories, modern motels, school wings, and barn stables, where a uniform sequence of repeated rooms is the organising idea rather than a centred common space.
placement_rules: Corridor orientation follows the building's long axis so that the room sequence reads end-to-end.
Keep corridor width at 2 blocks for residential and dormitory use; widen to 3 only for public-scale taverns or barn aisles.
Double-loaded corridors require the footprint to be at least corridor_width + 2 * min_room_depth wide (typically >= 8 blocks).
Single-loaded corridors place the corridor against the long external wall so all rooms face the same exterior side for light.
Terminate the corridor with a window or door on the short end to avoid a dead-end and to bring in daylight.
Doors to rooms should alternate along the corridor walls rather than sit directly opposite each other, preserving privacy between rooms across the hall.
Room count per side stays between 2 and 4 in domestic buildings; larger institutional variants extend the corridor rather than adding more rooms per side.
lighting_ventilation: Natural light enters from windows on the short end walls of the corridor, supplemented by transom openings or louvres above each room door. In darker double-loaded variants, place torches or lanterns every 4-5 blocks along the corridor ceiling or upper walls.

medieval.notes: Stone corridor walls with timber floor and ceiling beams; lit by iron lanterns or torches at regular intervals.

modern.notes: Clean white plaster walls; flush ceiling lights via sea_lantern or glowstone behind glass; uniform door spacing.

rustic.notes: All-timber corridor for farmstead dwellings and barn stable aisles; exposed spruce beams overhead.
